title: Mastery Exam: Forms, Validation, and Tables
summary: Mastery Exam: Forms, Validation, and Tables retrieves prior skills in an original independent heat-response release decision scenario and requires implementation, behavior, failure-state, and transfer evidence.
objectives: Apply form purpose method, form labels instructions, form input types, form grouping options, form native validation, table structure, table header associations, form table testing in unfamiliar constraints.
Produce or audit a real independent heat-response release decision artifact without happy-path-only reasoning.
Preserve earlier requirements while testing keyboard, narrow-width, invalid, and assistive-semantic states.

starterFiles.html: <!-- Continue the Heat Relief Network artifact here. -->

steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim survives the changed case? Scenario: independent heat-response release decision.
steps.why: Mastery Exam: Forms, Validation, and Tables makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 1 in independent heat-response release decision.

content.title: independent heat-response release decision evidence
content.text: For Mastery Exam: Forms, Validation, and Tables, choose the form purpose method claim that preserves the independent heat-response release decision purpose, native behavior, and understandable relationships when presentation changes.

hints: Mastery Exam: Forms, Validation, and Tables checkpoint 1: identify the user task and data relationship first.
Mastery Exam: Forms, Validation, and Tables checkpoint 1: compare source, native behavior, and announced meaning.
Mastery Exam: Forms, Validation, and Tables checkpoint 1: make the smallest repair that preserves earlier passing work.

options.text: Independent heat-response release decision case 1: a visible input value is submitted even when its control has no name.
Independent heat-response release decision case 1: choose action, method, and control names from the request workflow, then inspect the submitted name-value payload.
Independent heat-response release decision case 1: use POST for every form because method never changes browser or server behavior.

options.text: independent heat-response release decision phase 2.1: write a concise visible label for the requested value
independent heat-response release decision phase 2.2: give the control a unique id and match the label for value
independent heat-response release decision phase 2.3: place durable format or error guidance in visible text
independent heat-response release decision phase 2.4: connect guidance and verify name plus description in the accessibility tree

options.text: Independent heat-response release decision case 3: all short text values should use type text because other types only change appearance.
Independent heat-response release decision case 3: choose the narrowest truthful input type, add inputmode only when useful, and test real device keyboards plus valid edge cases.
Independent heat-response release decision case 3: use number for telephone numbers because every telephone number contains digits.

options.text: Independent heat-response release decision case 4: visually adjacent radio buttons automatically form an announced group.
Independent heat-response release decision case 4: choose radio, checkbox, or select from the choice relationship and expose group purpose with native structure.
Independent heat-response release decision case 4: use checkboxes for one required exclusive answer and repair multiple selections on the server.

options.text: Independent heat-response release decision case 5: a restrictive regular expression is always the strongest way to improve data quality.
Independent heat-response release decision case 5: use the least restrictive native constraints that match the real rule, explain failures clearly, and repeat validation on the server.
Independent heat-response release decision case 5: mark every control required so the service receives the largest possible data set.

options.text: Independent heat-response release decision case 6: tables are an efficient general-purpose tool for aligning page layout.
Independent heat-response release decision case 6: use a table only for two-dimensional data, give it a caption, and preserve row and column meaning without CSS.
Independent heat-response release decision case 6: use div elements with grid styling because visual columns automatically announce table relationships.

Independent heat-response release decision case 8: run an automated checker once and skip keyboard plus assistive-technology verification.
